coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of nickel oxide/copper cobaltite/graphene quantum dot conductive ink as an electrode material for selective electrochemical detection of epinephrine

coredata.dc:description: Wearable sensors and flexible electrodes are key components of point-of-care systems, facilitating enhanced accessibility and personalization in health monitoring. These technologies permit individuals to oversee their health status and enable healthcare professionals to remotely and in real time monitor patients’ health status more effectively. In this study, paper-based flexible screen-printed electrodes were constructed for the electrochemical determination of epinephrine (EP), which is crucial in the diagnosis of various diseases, with high selectivity and low detection limits. In a novel approach, nickel oxide (NiO), graphene quantum dots (GQD), and copper cobaltite (CuCo2O4) were employed in conjunctions for the first time in the development of conductive ink. The large surface area and high electrical conductivity of GQD, when combined with the electrocatalytic properties of NiO and CuCo2O4, resulted in the creation of a more efficient electroactive environment for electrochemical EP determination. The differential pulse voltammetry technique enabled the attainment of a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.66 nM within the 500–10 µM and 2–0.02 µM linear ranges. The reduction peak current of EP was examined throughout the analysis to eliminate the effects of possible interference species that may be present in real samples. Furthermore, the stable structures of GQDs and copper cobaltite enhanced the long-term performance of the sensors, and the combination of these materials increased the reproducibility and reliability of the sensors. Subsequently, the efficacy of the sensor was evaluated using real samples, specifically artificial sweat, and urine. The developed NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE sensor demonstrated satisfactory recovery values, with 94.5–110.5 %.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of nickel oxide/copper cobaltite/graphene quantum dot conductive ink as an electrode material for selective electrochemical detection of epinephrine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wearable sensors and flexible electrodes are key components of point-of-care systems, facilitating enhanced accessibility and personalization in health monitoring. These technologies permit individuals to oversee their health status and enable healthcare professionals to remotely and in real time monitor patients’ health status more effectively. In this study, paper-based flexible screen-printed electrodes were constructed for the electrochemical determination of epinephrine (EP), which is crucial in the diagnosis of various diseases, with high selectivity and low detection limits. In a novel approach, nickel oxide (NiO), graphene quantum dots (GQD), and copper cobaltite (CuCo2O4) were employed in conjunctions for the first time in the development of conductive ink. The large surface area and high electrical conductivity of GQD, when combined with the electrocatalytic properties of NiO and CuCo2O4, resulted in the creation of a more efficient electroactive environment for electrochemical EP determination. The differential pulse voltammetry technique enabled the attainment of a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.66 nM within the 500–10 µM and 2–0.02 µM linear ranges. The reduction peak current of EP was examined throughout the analysis to eliminate the effects of possible interference species that may be present in real samples. Furthermore, the stable structures of GQDs and copper cobaltite enhanced the long-term performance of the sensors, and the combination of these materials increased the reproducibility and reliability of the sensors. Subsequently, the efficacy of the sensor was evaluated using real samples, specifically artificial sweat, and urine. The developed NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE sensor demonstrated satisfactory recovery values, with 94.5–110.5 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Innovative developments in contemporary technology and healthcare are provided by flexible electrodes and wearable sensors. These sensors are flexible, lightweight devices that fit well on the body. Wearable sensors are typically used to track biological data in real-time. These gadgets are made to gather biometric information, which includes blood sugar levels, body temperature, heart rate, and even muscular movement [1]. Facilitates in progress biological data monitoring, offering significant advantages for tracking health and enhancing individual performance [2]. The ability to track numerous biomolecules, including glucose, lactate, and uric acid, in real time and at the appropriate location is crucial for good health [3].

The medulla of the adrenal glands produces adrenaline, a hormone and neurotransmitter that is vital to human health because it controls the body's reaction to stress and emergencies [4]. Epinephrine elevates blood sugar, quickens the heartbeat, and gets muscles ready for unexpected threats, all of which are components of the "war or what" response [5]. Both high and low adrenaline imbalances can have detrimental consequences on one's health, and aberrant adrenaline inflammation is the root cause of many disorders, including hypomania, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer's, schizophrenia, and Multiple Sclerosis [6]. Fatigue, low energy, and lack of motivation might result from low adrenaline. Addison's disease is particularly linked to adrenal insufficiency, a condition in which the body is unable to produce enough adrenaline. Hypoglycemia, a reduction in blood pressure, and muscle weakness may result from this. Conversely, high adrenaline levels can cause long-term stress, anxiety disorders, hypertension, irregular heartbeat, migraines, and trouble sleeping [7]. Therefore, the adrenaline molecule's balance is essential to general health and wellbeing. As a result, controlling and observing adrenaline levels is thought to be crucial to maintaining good health. Techniques including gas chromatography [8], high-performance liquid chromatography [9], liquid chromatology-tandem mass spectroscopy [10], fluorometry [11] and capillary electrophoresis [12] are among those listed as EP determination methods in literature. While these methods have benefits like high sensitivity, low detection limit, and high precision, they also have disadvantages like the need for a derivation process, complexity, and the need to purify non-volatile components. They can also be expensive, require many samples, and take a long time to analyze [13,14]. The use of electrochemical methods provides a quick and inexpensive substitute for the administration of adrenaline. These methods use epinephrine's electrochemical characteristics for analysis. These techniques have several benefits, including high precision, low detection limits, low cost, quick analysis periods, and small sample volumes. They can also be miniaturized, which makes them useful for on-site maintenance analyses (POCs) [15].

In recent years, various electrode materials have been employed in the literature for the electrochemical determination of the EP molecule. In their study, Spuka et al. utilized a carbon nanofiber-gold nanoparticles-modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE-CNF-AuNPs) to determine EP by square wave voltammetry. With a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.70 µM, they demonstrated that this method can be applied to human blood serum samples [16]. In a different study, a nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode was created using titanium dioxide-reduced graphene oxide nanofibers (TiO2-rGO NFs) for the electrochemical detection of EP, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 8.11 nM. It was proposed that this sensor could be used to determine EP in various fluid samples from the human body [17]. Kumar et al. modified a MoS2 heterostructure functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MW-CNTs) on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode using a hydrothermal route. The MoS2/MW-CNTs heterostructure modified GCE demonstrated excellent catalytic performance and proved instrumental in EP determination. The applicability of the method to real water samples was investigated, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.0 µM [18]. In further study, a screen-printed electrochemical sensor for the detection of EP was constructed based on reduced graphene doped poly(L-dopa)/PEDOT:PSS composites (poly(L-dopa)/rGO/PEDOT:PSS/SPCE) via a simple solution coating method and electro polymerization. The developed sensor was employed for the determination of EP in human blood serum samples, with a detection limit of 0.02 µM being achieved [19]. A review of the literature on the determination of EP reveals that while a wide variety of materials and modifying agents exhibiting high electrocatalytic properties have been used, most of the methods involve invasive real sample applications that require painful and time-consuming sampling procedures. Furthermore, the utilization of solid electrodes with arduous cleaning processes, such as GCE, is an impediment to the advancement of flexible and wearable sensor technologies, which are essential for local and timely point-of-care (POC) diagnostics. The use of screen-printed electrodes, which play a significant role in wearable sensor technologies, is frequently a commercially available and costly option. In light of these considerations, it is imperative to develop an electrode platform that is cost-effective, non-invasive, simple to prepare, flexible, and adaptable to wearable sensor technologies, and capable of being used in EP determination.

In a wide range of industries, such as biosensing, environmental monitoring, heavy metals, food and beverage safety, lab-to-field applications, pharmaceuticals, and wearable sensors, screen-printed electrodes are crucial. The utilization of screen-printed electrodes presents a multitude of benefits in electrochemical measurements, including cost-effectiveness, disposability, miniaturization and low volumes, multi-sensing, versatility, ease of manufacturing, compatibility, high reproducibility, portability, commercialization, ready to use, availability of different substrates and comparability [20]. Moreover, it offers the additional benefits of large-scale production capabilities and flexibility in manufacturing processes [21]. These electrodes are made of conductive inks that are usually printed onto flexible substrates using serigraphy [22]. The reference electrode, the working electrode, and the counter-electrode are the three primary parts of a SPE. This structure immediately impacts sensor performance by guaranteeing precise and consistent acquisition of the electrical signals needed for electrochemical analysis [23]. As a result, choosing the ink's constituent parts needs considerable thought.

Nickel oxide, a transition metal oxide, is one of the materials that is frequently utilized in electrochemical sensors. NiO is well-known for both its chemical stability and electrocatalytic activity. This material can enhance sensors' electrocatalytic performance because of its robust oxidation capabilities and large surface area. NiO is typically employed in electrochemical sensors to enhance their electrochemical performance and detect electroactive components [24]. Its abundance of resources makes it stand out and it is also environmentally beneficial. However, their limited electrical conductivity limits its potential for advanced sensor technology applications. Currently, this issue might be solved by combining the usage of materials with high conductivity, such carbon or graphene [25]. Because of their superior electrical and optical characteristics, graphene quantum dots, or GQDs, are zero-dimensional graphene structures with a variety of uses in electrochemical sensors. These materials exhibit strong photoluminescence characteristics, good electrical conductivity, and high surface area [26]. Because of their oxygen-rich functional groups, exceptional quantum capture, side effects, conductivity, stability, biocompatibility, and large surface area, GQDs are a great option for bio-perception electrodes. GQDs have a lot of carboxyl groups on their surface, which can greatly boost adrenaline's redox reaction [27].

In addition to these materials, the synergistic effects, low toxicity, low cost, good electrocatalysis performance, and outstanding electrical conductivity of spinel cobalt, which have emerged in recent years, represent high-performance materials in electrochemical sensors [28]. CuO and Co3O4 are frequently employed as modifying agents in electrochemical detection platforms in literature. A variety of techniques have been developed to enhance the active sites and electrical conductivity of Co3O4, including doping, regulating the growth of the metal phase, incorporating vacancies, and utilizing conductive support. These methods are further enhanced by the nanostructuring process, which creates a large reactive area with a high specific surface area. Consequently, these techniques have become widely utilized in numerous fields, including energy doping, sensing, solar cells, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) applications, oxygen evolution reaction (OER), gas sensors, and photocatalysts [29,30]. Combining Cu2+ and Co3+ can greatly enhance electrochemical properties because of the high conductivity of copper compounds and the strong electrochemical activity of cobalt compounding [31]. CuCo2O4 has been utilized in the electrochemical determination of numerous biomolecules and is a promising catalyst for electrochemical sensor applications and biological detection systems [32,33]. It is believed that the combination of these three substances will show a high degree of electrochemical reactivity on the electrode surface when used to measure adrenaline.

This study produced paper-based, flexible screen-printed electrodes for the electrochemical determination of epinephrine with differential pulse voltammetry. The conductive ink was created, and optimal ratios were found by assessing the EP designation's performance and it contains nickel oxide, graphene quantum dots, and copper cobaltite. The produced NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE has been characterized chemically, morphologically, and electrochemically. Using analytical techniques, the LOD value was determined to be 11.66 nM. It was discovered that the interference study's impact on the categories of interferences analysis was less than 5 %. Long-term performance of the sensors was improved by the stable structures of the GQDs in the SPE structure and CuCo2O4; the combination of these materials also improved the repeatability and dependability of the sensors. The NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE sensor that was created using simulated sweat and urine demonstrated good recovery values ranging from 94.5 % to 106 % in real sample studies.

The Supplementary Material section contains comprehensive information about reagents, and all the substances used in the experiments are of analytical grade, include specifications and information about the instruments used in all characterization studies, comprehensive details on every electrochemical measurement that was used in the experiments.

The sol-gel method, which has been previously documented in the literature, was employed to synthesize NiO [34]. This was accomplished by transferring 1.5 g of NiCl2 .6H2O to a 250 mL round bottom flask, dissolving it at room temperature in 70 mL of pure ethanol, and producing a light green solution. Separately, NaOH (0.5 g) is dissolved in 100 mL of absolute ethanol. This solution has been added to the solution of nickel chloride by droplets. For two hours, the mixture is stirred at room temperature. It has been observed that the reaction mixture forms a light green gel during this period. The gel was filtered, then completely washed with ethanol and deionized water after two hours. By air-drying the precipitate, a light green powder was produced. Ultimately, a 30-min calcination at 290 °C produced a black powder.

The preparation of the GQD involved adjusting the degree of carbonization of citric acid in accordance with earlier publications [35]. In short, 2 g of citric acid were heated to 200 °C for 5 min to produce a colorless liquid, and then the heat was applied steadily for another 30 min or so until the color turned orange. Subsequently, 100 mL of a 10 mg/mL sodium hydroxide solution was mixed vigorously and continuously with the orange liquid that had been obtained. Ultimately, 1 mg/mL of citric acid solution was added to the GQD aqueous solution to bring its pH level down to 8.0. For later usage, the GQD solution was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Following a few minor modifications, CuCo2O4 nanoparticles were created via the co-precipitation approach, as has been previously documented in the literature [36]. In typical synthesis, 2.5 g of Cu(CH2COO)2 .H2O and 2.5 g of Co(CH2COO)2 .4H2O precursor metals were mixed in 125 mL of pure water until they were completely dissolved (Solution 1). Then, 4.25 g of C2H2O4·2H2O was weighed and mixed in 60 mL of pure water until homogeneous (Solution 2). Then Solution 2 was added dropwise to Solution 1 slowly. After the addition of C2H2O4 .2H2O, the total solution is stirred for 20 min. The resulting precipitate is centrifuged until pH 7.0. It is dried at 70 °C overnight and calcinated at 550 °C for 4 h.

First, the solvent system, comprising 1.25 mL deionized water, 1.25 mL ethanol, and 0.7 mL GQD, was mixed with 125 mg CMC and stirred magnetically at 1500 rpm and room temperature until complete dissolution of the CMC had occurred. Subsequently, 93.75 mg of NiO nanoparticles were introduced and mixed until a sufficient dispersion was achieved. Then, 93.75 mg of CuCo2O4 nanoparticles were introduced. Finally, 500 mg of graphite powder was introduced to the solution and mechanically stirred for a period of 24 hours. The resulting conductive ink was prepared for printing without further steps.

Technical designs of various mask types, each consisting of different layers for screen-printed electrodes, were created using the Siemens NX program. Using a printer (Silhouette Cameo 3, Austria), the masks were manufactured. By applying a squeegee, the conductive inks were printed onto the paper substrates. The chemical composition of each electrode varies in screen-printed electrodes (SPEs), which are three-electrode systems. As a percentage by weight, the conductive ink used for the counter electrode was made up of 80 % Graphite and 20 % CMC. The reference electrode was made with silver paste that was sold commercially. The working electrode for the determination of epinephrine was printed using conductive ink containing NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD, for which the optimum conditions were established. After being annealed for 60 min at 150 °C, the electrodes were considered fit for use. The electrode prepared in accordance with the steps was named as the NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE. The process used to create paper-based flexible electrodes that were designed for the electrochemical measurement of epinephrine is shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conductive ink composition and annealing parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The conductive components of the ink formulation, its binder and solvent have an influence on the behavior of the electrodes that are prepared by the screen-printing technique. The materials used also affect the conductivity, morphology and electrochemical activity of the dried layers. Therefore, it is imperative to optimize these materials, especially in the context of complex and multilayer systems.

Seven different solvent systems were formulated by varying the ratios of water (W) and ethanol (E). The W:E ratios (v/v) were 2.5:0.0, 2.0:0.5, 1.5:1.0, 1.25:1.25, 1.0:1.5, 0.5:2.0, and 0.0:2.5, respectively. In each solvent system, 125 mg of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), 500 mg graphite, 93.75 mg NiO, 93.75 mg CuCo2O4, and 0.5 mL GQD were added and allowed to stir until solubilization was complete. In the solvent systems containing 0.5 W:2.0E and 0.0 W:2.5E, the CMC was not fully dissolved, thus precluding the preparation of conductive inks. The differential pulse voltammograms presented in Fig. 2a illustrate the impact of solvent ratios on EP determination. As the proportion of ethanol in the solvent system is increased, the reduction peak current rises. The highest reduction peak current of −1.22 µA at −0.24 V was obtained with an ink prepared with a 1.25 W:1.25E solvent system. This increase in peak current with increasing ethanol content can be attributed to the good dispersion of the metal particles in the ink formulation [37]. A further plausible explanation could be that the solvent system's low boiling point speeds up the annealing process. At lower temperatures, as the solvent is completely evaporated, the contact area between conductive materials and the creation of a conducting network rises. On the other hand, CMC, an insulating and water-soluble binder, does not dissolve as well in a solution where the concentration of ethanol is greater than that of water, which prevents the development of ideal conductive pathways [38].

The entire solid loading was tuned to provide the ideal viscosity and consistency in the conductive ink. To do this, the conductive ink composition's amounts of CMC, NiO, copper cobaltite, and GQD were kept constant. The only difference was in the overall weight since graphite was added. A range of conductive inks with total solid loadings of 300 mg, 400 mg, 500 mg, 600 mg, and 700 mg were used to create SPEs. The best result for determining EP was obtained when SPEs were made using an ink containing 500 mg of graphite, as Fig. 2b shows. This can be linked to the improved development of the particle network in the binder as a result of the higher solid loading and equilibrium [39].

As a binder, CMC, a substance with a high percentage of -OH groups, was used. There were five different conductive inks made, with 45 mg, 85 mg, 125 mg, 165 mg, and 205 mg of CMC in each. Next, their effect on EP oxidation was examined. The electrochemical response of SPEs made with conductive inks containing different amounts of CMC and graphite is shown in Fig. 2c. When there is too much binder, the conducting particles in the conductive filler can't make good contact with one other, which breaks the continuous connection. A drop in current occurs when there is a gap between the conductive particles due to the high concentration of non-conductive binder. On the other hand, when the binder content decreases, the conductive particles become more grouped together and form a conductive network, increasing the maximum electric current [40]. A good oxidation peak for EP was found in the conductive ink, which contained 125 mg CMC, when the voltammogram was analyzed. This ratio indicates the formation of an ideal conductive network structure and the achievement of a conductive ink with good dispersion.

To find out what effect the GQD concentration in the conductive ink had, conductive inks with different amounts of GQD were made. GQD concentrations of 0.1 mL, 0.3 mL, 0.5 mL, 0.7 mL, 0.9 mL, and 1.1 mL were used to produce the inks. Fig. 2d displays the voltammograms obtained by SPEs made with these inks. Using inks containing 0.7 mL GQD, the greatest peak current of −1.45 µA at −0.24 V was achieved to estimate the electrochemical potential. The enhanced surface area and superior electrical conductivity of GQD are responsible for the noted rise in peak current values [41]. Conversely, while the quantity of GQD augmented, the peak current diminished after a specific threshold was reached. It is hypothesized that an increase in the quantity of GQDs results in supersaturation at the electrode surface. Furthermore, it is postulated that the aggregation of a considerable number of GQDs may result in a reduction in surface efficiency due to the manifestation of an agglomeration tendency [42].

The impact of varying CuCo2O4 concentrations in the conductive ink formulation on the ability to determine the EP was examined. SPEs produced with conductive inks containing varied concentrations of CuCo2O4, that 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 % of total solid amount, were used to achieve the voltammograms shown in Fig. 2e. The best oxidation peak was found at −1.45 μA and −0.24 V when 15 % CuCo2O4 containing conductive ink was used. At one-point, peak current was found to decrease in proportion to the increase in CuCo2O4 %. To investigate the effect of NiO content, six different conductive inks were prepared, containing 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 % NiO, and SPEs were produced. The resulting measurement results are presented in Fig. 2f. As can be seen, the maximum peak current was obtained with the ink containing 15 % NiO. Thereafter, the peak current decreased with increasing NiO content. A significant attribute of conductive ink is the stability of the metal particles produced. Due to the elevated energy state of the metal particles in the conductive ink, sedimentation, agglomeration, and aggregation occur spontaneously [43,44]. The generation of stable conductive inks is significantly impeded by these three laborious procedures. These parameters can be utilized to elucidate the reduction in current that occurs when the quantities of CuCo2O4 and NiO increase.

More research is needed on the annealing process, which is required to create a continuous conducting network on the surface of printed electrodes. To achieve this, the effects of different annealing temperatures (60, 90, 120, 150, 180, and 210 °C) as well as different annealing periods (15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, and 105 min) at 150 °C were investigated. The conductive ink particles are brought closer together when the solvent evaporates after the ink is printed onto paper substrates. A steady and unbroken percolation network forms along the printed path upon contact between the previously described particles [45]. Particles with a tiny initial contact area have thicker necks because of the annealing process. The degree of contact between the particles increases with temperature, leading to a proportional increase in conductivity. This modification leads to an increase in peak currents for SPE. A rise in the peak current of EP reduction is noted when raising the annealing temperature from 60 °C to 150 °C (Fig. 2g). At 150 °C, the peak current behaved optimally; however, as the temperature rose, decreasing until it reached lower current values. This behavior can be explained by the paper substrate's brittleness at higher temperatures [46]. The conclusion was that 150 °C was the temperature that offered the best annealing conditions. In a similar vein, testing annealing periods ranging from 15 to 105 min, inclusive, revealed that 60 min was the ideal annealing time (Fig. 2h). Its appropriateness for the annealing process was indicated by the highest peak current.

The morphology and elemental composition of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE were investigated utilizing EDX and FESEM. Fig. 3a-f illustrates the FESEM images of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE at varying magnifications, including 10,000x, 25,000x, 50,000x, and 100,000x. A higher magnification image provides greater clarity regarding the agglomerated structures. Upon analysis of the morphology, it becomes evident that prominent graphite-induced layers and flat broken plates are present within the structure [46]. In addition, the NiO particles within the formulation exhibit cube-like structures with a discernible edge structure [47]. In another notable observation, CuCo2O4 particles were found to exhibit nanorod structures, a finding that aligns with previous reports in the literature [48]. As is evident from the FESEM images, the aggregation effects observed in these nanoparticles can be attributed to their intrinsic magnetic properties, specifically those of CuCo2O4 [49].

Fig. 4a illustrates the EDX overall mapping results of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE. As illustrated in Fig. 4b-f, the EDX mapping results demonstrate the presence of oxygen, carbon, nickel, copper, and cobalt, with respective atomic percentages of 28.8, 61.9, 7.1, 1.5, and 1.5 %.

The cyclic voltammograms and Nyquist diagrams of different SPEs using G/SPE, GQD/G/SPE, CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, and NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE are detailed in Fig. 5a and b. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, a representative redox peak for Fe(CN)6 3-/4- has been discernible for all SPEs. The peak potential difference (ΔEp) for G/SPE was determined to be 0.301 V (Epa = +0.283 V and Epc = −0.018 V). Additionally, the anodic and cathodic peak currents were found to be 7.99 µA and −8.66 µA, respectively. In comparison to the G/SPE, the ΔEp value on the GQD/G/SPE decreased to 0.081 V (Epa = +0.161 V and Epc = +0.080 V). There was also a decrease in the oxidation and reduction peak currents (Ipa = 4.94 µA and Ipc = −3.89 µA). In the case of CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, the ΔEp value increased to 0.327 V (Epa = +0.303 V and Epc = −0.024 V), accompanied by a notable rise in the background current. Additionally, the oxidation and reduction peak currents increased to 17.06 μA and −15.72 μA, respectively. Compared to CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, ΔEp on NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE decreased to 0.203 V (Epa = +0.241 V and Epc = +0.038 V), with a peak current ratio (Ipa/Ipc) close to 1:1 (Ipa = 29.35 μA and Ipc = −29.36 μA) on NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE. Consequently, the elevated peak current values of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE in comparison to G/SPE can be ascribed to the enhanced surface area. Furthermore, it can be concluded that the reaction on the NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE surface facilitates a more rapid electron transfer due to a reduction in ΔEp [50].

A comparison of the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and capacitance values (C) of various SPEs, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, offers valuable insight into the data. The relationship between C and Rct serves as the foundation for electrochemical systems in the context of electrochemical reactions. The term "capacitance" is used to describe the ability of an electrode/electrolyte interface to store charge. In contrast, the term "charge transfer resistance" is used to describe the difficulty of charge transfer during electrochemical reactions. A low Rct is typically associated with higher C values, indicating that the system can store and transfer charge more efficiently [51]. Moreover, the correlation between C and Rct can be influenced by the intrinsic properties of the electrodes. Materials with a high surface area and favorable electronic characteristics can result in a lower Rct and a higher C, thereby enhancing the overall electrochemical performance [52,53]. The Rct values for GQD/G/SPE, G/SPE, CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, and NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE are presented in descending order: 1.59 kΩ, 1.31 kΩ, 0382 kΩ, and 0366 kΩ, respectively. In contrast, the capacitance values (C) of each electrode were determined to be 2.40×10−9, 3.46×10−9, 1.42×10−8, and 1.80×10−8 F, respectively. These results indicate that the electrode prepared with NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G composite conductive ink facilitates the electron transfer of redox probes, exhibits exemplary electrochemical properties and the Rct and C values are compatible [50]. Furthermore, it is evident that the Rs value obtained in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl solution using GQD/G/SPE is distinct from those obtained with alternative electrodes. It is postulated that this discrepancy may be attributed to the characteristics of GQD, its interaction with iron ions, and the broader electrochemical environment resulting from its extensive surface area.

Fig. 5c illustrates the FTIR spectrum of the conductive ink comprising G, NiO, GQD, and CuCo2O4. The highest peak is observed in the wavenumber range of 3440 cm−1, which represents the stretching vibrations of the hydroxyl (O-H) group. Furthermore, the wavenumber range of 2361 cm−1, and 1625 cm−1 also exhibits the presence of carbonyl groups (CO) and aromatic rings (CC), respectively. In the meantime, at 1380 cm−1 wavenumber, there is another CO bond. Additionally, aliphatic group (C-H) bonds are identified at a wave number of 2925 cm−1 [54]. Generally, FTIR transmission spectrum characteristics were seen below 1000 cm−1, which is in line with metal-oxygen bond vibration. The stretching vibrations of Co3+-O2- tetrahedral complexes and Cu2+-O2- octahedral complexes, respectively, are represented by the two peaks in CuCo2O4 at 568 and 669 cm−1. These values are explained by CuCo2O4 fingerprint IR absorptions [28,55]. The peak observed at 1104 and 556 and 657 cm−1 indicated the formation of the metal-oxide (Ni-O) bond, which allowed for the configuration of NiO to be recognized [56,57]. Moreover, this finding is corroborated by the XRD and XPS data.

Fig. 5d illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of a conductive ink powder formulated with G, GQD, NiO, and CuCo2O4. The XRD patterns demonstrate the presence of the primary characteristic peaks associated with the materials comprising the structure. Given the presence of multiple materials within the structure, minor fluctuations in the 2θ values are discernible. The graphite matches the peak at 2θ values of 26.46° [58]. The diffraction peaks observed at 43.24°, 62.64°, and 75.11° 2θ values correspond to the formation of NiO nanoparticle structures [59]. The 2θ values of the CuCo2O4 structure were observed to be 18.92°, 31.18°, 36.75°, 38.72°, 44.77°, 54.56°, 59.21°, 65.14°, and 77.41°, respectively. The remaining CuO and Co3O4 impurities within the CuCo2O4 structure were identified at 2θ values of 35.49°, 48.53°, 58.18°, and 61.46° for CuO and 55.56°, 65.97°, and 77.64° for Co3O4, respectively [36]. Despite the presence of impurities in the structure, the peak intensities indicate that each material has been successfully synthesized and incorporated into the conductive ink formulation, with no discernible alteration to the crystal structure.

Fig. 6 depicts the XPS spectrum of the conductive ink, which contains G, NiO, GQD, and CuCo2O4. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the XPS survey spectrum exhibits distinctive peaks at 933.20, 854.04, 779.20, 533.16, and 284.46 eV, which correspond to the Cu2p, Ni2p, Co2p, O1s, and C1s orbitals, respectively. Fig. 6b illustrates the presence of peaks attributed to Ni2+ at approximately 855.67 eV for Ni2p3/2 (satellite peak at 861.18 eV) and approximately 873.35 eV for Ni2p1/2 (satellite peak at 879.21 eV). Additionally, minor peaks at 854.02 and 871.21 eV are attributed to low Ni3+ content [60]. The XPS spectrum of Cu2p is illustrated in Fig. 6c. The Cu2p spectrum exhibits a peak for Cu2p3/2 at approximately 933.38 eV, which is attributed to Cu2+. The satellite peak of Cu2p3/2 was observed at an energy value of 942.09 eV. Additionally, a peak for Cu2p1/2 is observed at approximately 953.40 eV, corresponding to Cu2p1/2. The satellite peak of Cu2p1/2 was observed to occur at approximately 959.14 eV. These findings are in accordance with those observed for Cu2+ in the CuCo2O4 structure [36]. Fig. 6d illustrates that the Co2p1/2 peak can be decomposed into two distinct peaks, occurring at 796.32 eV and 794.21 eV, respectively. Concurrently, the Co2p3/2 peak can be decomposed into two peaks at 780.76 eV and 779.18 eV. The peaks observed at 796.32 eV and 780.76 eV are attributed to the Co2+ state, while the peaks observed at 794.21 eV and 779.18 eV are attributed to the Co3+ state [61]. In conclusion, XPS analysis successfully identified the presence of Cu2+, Co2+, Co3+, and Ni2+ in the conductive ink formulation. This observation is consistent with the XPS results previously reported in the literature for NiO and CuCo2O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of EP on the NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of each nanomaterial into the ink formulation was evaluated for its influence on EP reduction and its effect on sensor performance. Cyclic voltammograms and differential pulse voltammograms were recorded in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS, 0.1 M KCl, and 0.5 mM EP ( Fig. 7a and b). Upon analysis of the cyclic voltammograms of G/SPE, GQD/G/SPE, CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, and NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE, it becomes evident that NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE exhibits the highest reduction peak current, with a value of −3.35 μA. Similarly, the highest reduction peak current of −2.82 μA was obtained with NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE from the differential pulse voltammograms. The results are deemed to be satisfactory. The incorporation of GQD into the ink formulation resulted in not only an enhancement in the peak current, attributable to its elevated conductivity and surface area, but also an improvement in the peak shape. Subsequently, the optimal peak current was obtained with the addition of CuCo2O4 and NiO materials to the structure. This can be attributed to the favorable electrocatalysis performance exhibited by the materials and the enhanced surface area.

The impact of pH on the electro-reduction of epinephrine on a NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE surface was examined using the DPV technique in a 0.5 mM EP solution prepared in phosphate buffers with varying pH values (10.4–4.4). The differential pulse voltammograms obtained are presented in Fig. 7c, while the Ep-pH plot is provided in Fig. 7d. As illustrated in the voltammogram, the Ep values decrease as the pH value increases. This suggests that EP oxidation is pH dependent [62]. Upon examination of the relationship between Ep and pH, it was observed that the line equation obtained was Ep = −0.0546 pH +0.1296, R2 = 0.9950, with a slope of −0.0546. When this value is nearly equal to the theoretical value in the Nernst equation (-0.059), it indicates that the number of electrons and protons transferred during the electro-reduction of EP are equal [63].

Fig. 8a illustrates the cyclic voltammograms obtained successively in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS and 0.1 M KCl containing 0.5 mM EP. In this instance, voltammogram a (red line) represents the initial cycle, while voltammogram b (black dot) represents the second cycle. The O1 peak observed here represents the oxidation of epinephrine to epinephrine quinone. The epinephrine quinone formed exhibits two distinct states. It can either remain intact or undergo an irreversible 1,4 intramolecular cyclization, accompanied by a nucleophilic attack on the nitrogen atom (Michael addition). This reaction results in the formation of leucoepinephrine quinone, an unstable species that is rapidly oxidized to epinephrine quinone [63]. The R1 reduction peak observed in the voltammogram can be attributed to the reduction of previously formed epinephrine chrome to leucoepinephrine chrome (Fig. 8b). The O2 peak observed in the second scan is indicative of the oxidation of leucoepinephrine chrome, which is formed because of R2 [62]. In consideration of the entirety of the reaction mechanism, the peak at which epinephrine chrome is reduced to leucoepinephrine chrome was employed for the electrochemical determination of EP. One of the primary objectives of conducting an analysis using the reduction peak is to effectively eliminate the interference effect.

Cyclic voltammograms of a 0.5 mM EP solution on NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE at increasing scan rates were recorded and are presented in Fig. S1a. Upon detailed analysis of the recorded voltammograms, it was observed that the cathodic peak potential exhibited a slight positive trend, while the cathodic peak current demonstrated a linear increase with the rise in scan rate. Fig. S1b, c, and d, illustrate the v-Ip, v1/2-Ip, and log(v)-log(Ip) graphs, respectively. The equations of the graphs plotted are as follows:
(1) Ip(µA) = -0.51 v(mV/s) - 0.768, R2 = 0.9827
(2) Ip(µA) = -0.561v1/2 + 0.657, R2 = 0.9993
(3) log(Ip) = 0.6626logv – 0.6054, R2 = 0.9978

The v1/2-Ip plot (Eq.(2)) is more linear than the v-Ip plot (Eq. (1)), and the slope of the log(v)- log(Ip) plot (Eq. (3)) is not close to 1. These findings indicate that electron transfer on handmade NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE is predominantly diffusion controlled [64].

The effects of NiO, GQD, and CuCo2O4 nanomaterials on the growth of the electroactive surface area were evaluated. By using CV measurement at different scan rates in an aqueous solution containing 1 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4 and 0.1 M KCl, the electroactive surface areas of G/SPE and NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE were ascertained (Fig. S2a and Fig. S3a). The following equation (Eq. (4)) presents the Randles–Sevcik relationship [46].
(4)
Ip = 2.69 x
10
5
n
1 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
*
ν
1 / 2

The term Ip is defined as the peak current of the redox couple. In this context, n represents the number of electrons transferred, D denotes the diffusion coefficient of the solution (6.7×10−5 cm2/s at 25 °C), C* signifies the molarity of the solution (mol/L), and ν refers to the scan rate (V/s). Additionally, A is the electrode area (cm2). Based on the slope values of the plot between Ip and v1/2 (Fig. S2b and S3b), the electroactive surface areas of G/SPE and NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE were computed as 2.92×10−7 cm2 and 5.92×10−7 cm2, respectively. The observed rise in active surface area can be attributed to the presence of NiO, GQD, and CuCo2O4 nanoparticles in the conductive ink formulation. These nanoparticles enhance the porosity of the electroactive surface and, consequently, the surface area.

The reduction of EP molecules at physiological pH was facilitated by SPEs made with conductive ink composed of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G, the optimal composition of which was identified. Based on the DPV results, Fig. 9a shows a linear increase in peak current when EP concentration increases between 0.02 and 500 μM. In the range of 0.02–2 µM and 10–500 µM for EP concentration, two calibration curves were produced (Fig. 9b).

Ip(μA) = −0.0038 C(μM) - 0.9701 is the calibration curve equation for the concentration range of 0.02–2 µM. The value of correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9986. The calibration curve equation Ip(μA) = −0.3621 C(μM) - 0.244 was derived for the concentration range of 10–500 µM. It had a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9932. The electrode's limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were determined using equation (Eq. (5)):
(5)
Limit of detection = K
x
S
m

The definition of K is the signal to noise ratio (S/N), S is the lowest measurable concentration value's standard deviation, and m is the calibration curve's slope. For the LOD calculation, the S/N was set to 3, and for the LOQ calculation, to 10 [65]. By employing the provided equation, LOD and LOQ were ascertained to be 11.66 nM and 38.88 nM, respectively.

Upon examination of the calibration graph obtained from solutions with varying EP concentrations, two distinct linear ranges were observed at high and low concentrations, respectively, as a result of measurements taken with NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE. It is important to note that concentration is not the sole parameter that can influence the response of EP in measurements taken with NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE. It is established that there is a linear correlation between the active surface area of the electrode and the peak current [66]. The reason for this phenomenon may be that the active surface area of the electrode has reached saturation at a certain point, thereby limiting the availability of the active area for higher concentrations. Alternatively, the analyte may induce an ohmic drop at specific concentrations. Despite the apparent presence of two distinct linear ranges in the graph, this is because the analyte signal is received at a level that is less than the expected linear value [46].

The NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE electrode demonstrates considerable promise for the electrochemical detection of EP, as evidenced by the data presented in Table 1. The electrode exhibits superior analytical performance compared to many electrodes documented in the literature. These attributes are a consequence of the electrode's low LOD value, applicability at physiological pH, and linear range. Furthermore, the electrode is entirely handmade, disposable, and cost-effective. Furthermore, the sensitivity data from several studies referenced in the literature are presented in Table 1. The NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G sensor was developed for the determination of EP, and a sensitivity value of 0.3621 μM/μA was obtained within the linear range of 0.02–0.2 μM. As can be observed, this value is higher than that of many other studies presented in the table. These findings suggest that electrochemical determination of EP can be conducted with high precision using NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G.

The selectivity of NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G/SPE towards EP was investigated in the presence of a range of interfering species that are commonly found in real samples, including uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), folic acid (FA), creatinine (Cre), glucose (Glu), urea (U), sodium chloride (NaCl), and potassium chloride (KCl). Fig. 9c illustrates the voltammogram, which depicts the measurement of a solution containing 100 µM EP (black line), a solution containing the same amount of interfering species as EP (red line), and a solution containing 10 times more concentrated interfering species than EP (blue line). In the absence of interfering species, the reduction peak current value of EP was −1.37 μA. In the presence of interfering species at equal concentration and 10-fold concentration, the peak current values were −1.41 μA and −1.43 μA, respectively. The resulting values were then used to calculate the percentage error, which was found to be 2.92 % and 4.38 %, respectively. It can thus be concluded that the presence of various species in real samples does not significantly interfere with the electrochemical determination of EP with NiO/CuCo2O4/GQDG/SPE. The fact that the electrochemical determination was conducted on the reduction peak has a significant impact on the absence of a notable interference effect.

Additionally, the shift in the peak potential of EP towards a more anodic region was noted in the presence of interfering species. It is clearly seen that the peak potential is −0.25 V in the absence of interference species, −0.19 V in the presence of species at the same concentration and −0.11 V in the presence of 10 times more concentrated species. This shift in the peak potential of EP is thought to be due to kinetic effects due to the presence of interfering species, changes in electrode surface properties and concentration-dependent interactions. The primary cause of the shift in the peak potential is the kinetic effect resulting from the coexistence of interfering species [72]. Furthermore, the configuration of the electrode surface can markedly influence the peak potential. The presence of specific species can modify the surface characteristics of the electrode, resulting in alterations in charge transfer rates [73]. As evidenced by the pH study, the peak potential of EP is contingent upon the pH of the medium (Fig. 7c). The acidic milieu resulting from the addition of ascorbic acid and uric acid to the medium in the interference study may precipitate a shift in the peak potential towards the anodic region. This indicates that the interaction between the analyte and the electrode surface is contingent upon not only the analyte itself, but also the surrounding chemical environment.

Five SPEs that were manufactured under identical conditions were used for DPV measurements in EP solution to examine the repeatability of the sensor. With an RSD value of 1.68 %, the sensor exhibits good reproducibility, according to the results shown in Fig. S4. To verify the stability of the sensor with 1 μM EP solution on days 1, 7, 14, 21, and 28, DPV measurements were conducted using electrodes manufactured under the same conditions. On day 14, Fig. 9d depicts a decline in the EP reduction peak current with an inaccuracy of more than 5 %. Nevertheless, over the course of a week, the sensor demonstrated consistent stability.

The practical utility of the improved electrode was demonstrated by measuring EP levels in a synthetic sweat and urine sample produced using methodologies previously reported in the literature [74,75]. Current responses were recorded when standard EP solution was added to the artificial sweat sample and urine samples at doses of 1 μM, 2 μM, 100 μM and 300 μM. According to the calibration plot, an increase in the reduction peak current was seen with each addition of EP. The computed values for the relative standard deviation and recovery are shown in Table 2. The flexible sensor's recovery value fell between 94.5 % and 110.5 %.

Real-time patient monitoring is made possible by the development of flexible electrodes and wearable sensors as essential parts of POC systems that improve accessibility and customization in health monitoring. In this work, for the electrochemical detection of EP paper-based flexible screen-printed electrodes with low detection limits and good selectivity were created. GQD, CuCo2O4, and NiO were combined in a novel way to create conductive ink. Various techniques were used to evaluate the NiO/CuCo2O4/GQD/G conductive ink and demonstrate the synthesis and presence of nanomaterials in the ink formulation. Given the advantageous chemical properties of these materials and their potential integration into flexible and wearable technology, the development of EP sensors using them may be of interest. These materials may prove to be an optimal choice for EP determination due to their high surface area, high catalytic activity, good conductivity, and repeatability. Moreover, these sensors can be utilized for a multitude of applications in daily living and health monitoring due to their adaptable nature. The development and integration of such sensors into a multitude of healthcare technology applications in the future could significantly advance the field of healthcare technology.
